threats.title: ETH Fallback in _transferOutAndCallV5 Sends Funds to Aggregator Target Instead of User Recipient
threats.hypothesis_description: When the aggregator's swapOutV5 call fails in the ETH path of _transferOutAndCallV5, the fallback sends ETH to `aggregationPayload.target` (the aggregator contract) instead of `aggregationPayload.recipient` (the intended user). This is a regression from V4's transferOutAndCall which correctly falls back to the recipient (`to`). Any failed aggregator swap results in user ETH being sent to an unintended contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect visible by comparing V4 and V5 fallback logic. The V4 `transferOutAndCall` fallback sends to `to` (user), while V5 sends to `target` (aggregator). Failed swaps are a normal operational occurrence (slippage, liquidity issues), making this a high-frequency path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Wrong recipient in fallback: sends to aggregator target instead of user recipient

preconditions: Vault calls transferOutAndCallV5 or batchTransferOutAndCallV5 with ETH
Aggregator's swapOutV5 call fails (slippage, liquidity, revert)
aggregationPayload.target != aggregationPayload.recipient

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: User's ETH is sent to aggregator contract on swap failure instead of being returned to user
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that target == recipient, no recipient fallback

analysis_questions: How frequently do aggregator swapOutV5 calls fail in production?
Do all whitelisted aggregators have rescue/withdrawal mechanisms?
Can an aggregator intentionally fail to keep ETH?
validation_requirements: Deploy test with failing aggregator and verify ETH goes to target not recipient
Compare V4 and V5 fallback behavior side by side
Quantify ETH at risk based on historical failed swap frequency

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault sends ETH via transferOutAndCallV5. Aggregator swap fails due to slippage. ETH is sent to aggregator contract instead of user. If aggregator has no withdrawal mechanism or owner is unresponsive, user funds are permanently lost.
prerequisites: Active vault performing ETH swaps via aggregator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full ETH amount of any failed aggregator swap is misdirected
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user receiving ETH via cross-chain swap through an aggregator
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on aggregator having rescueFunds; no router-side recovery

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying fallback recipient in V5 ETH path vs V4

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing TransferOutAndCallV5 event with on-chain ETH flow

threats.title: ERC20 Tokens Irrecoverably Lost When Aggregator swapOutV5 Fails in _transferOutAndCallV5
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferOutAndCallV5 ERC20 path, tokens are transferred to the aggregator via `transfer()`, then `swapOutV5` is called. If swapOutV5 fails, the return value `_dexAggSuccess` is completely ignored and no fallback exists. Tokens remain stranded at the aggregator with no router-side recovery mechanism. The transaction succeeds and emits events as if the operation completed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code comment explicitly acknowledges this design ('if the aggregator fails, it should not revert the whole transaction') but provides no recovery path. Unlike the ETH path which has a fallback (albeit to the wrong address), the ERC20 path has zero fallback logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unchecked return value after token transfer; no recovery on failure

preconditions: Vault calls _transferOutAndCallV5 with ERC20 fromAsset
ERC20 transfer to aggregator succeeds
Aggregator swapOutV5 fails for any reason

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Tokens transferred to aggregator, then swap called, failure silently ignored
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _dexAggSuccess is declared but never used in any conditional logic
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback transfer to recipient, no revert on failure, no recovery mechanism

analysis_questions: What percentage of aggregator swaps fail in production?
Do all whitelisted aggregators implement rescueFunds for ERC20?
Could a malicious aggregator be whitelisted and intentionally fail swaps to steal tokens?
validation_requirements: Test with THORChain_Failing_Aggregator to confirm tokens are stranded
Verify there's no secondary mechanism to recover tokens
Check if Bifrost detects and retries failed aggregator swaps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault sends ERC20 via _transferOutAndCallV5. Aggregator's swapOutV5 reverts. Tokens are already at aggregator. Transaction succeeds, event emitted, but user never receives anything. Tokens locked at aggregator.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full ERC20 amount of any failed aggregator swap is stranded
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user receiving ERC20 via cross-chain aggregated swap
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only if aggregator has rescueFunds accessible to owner

threats.title: Aggregator swapIn Deposits Entire Contract ETH Balance Including Pre-existing Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: In THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn, after the DEX swap, `_safeAmount = address(this).balance` captures the entire ETH balance of the aggregator contract, not just the output of the current swap. Any pre-existing ETH (from direct sends, failed operations, or other sources) is attributed to the current caller's deposit. An attacker can steal any ETH sitting in the aggregator by calling swapIn with minimal tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The aggregator has a `receive() external payable` function allowing anyone to send ETH to it. Failed ETH fallbacks from router operations could also leave ETH in the aggregator. The use of address(this).balance instead of tracking the delta means all accumulated ETH is swept into the attacker's deposit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses total contract balance instead of swap output delta

preconditions: Aggregator contract holds pre-existing ETH/AVAX
Attacker has some ERC20 tokens to initiate a swap
DEX has liquidity for the token->WETH pair

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: address(this).balance includes pre-existing ETH, not just swap output
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: All ETH in aggregator is deposited under caller's memo
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pre/post balance delta tracking for ETH

analysis_questions: How does ETH accumulate in the aggregator in practice?
Is this exploitable atomically (send ETH + call swapIn in same tx)?
Does the AvaxAggregator have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to aggregator, then call swapIn with dust tokens
Verify the deposit includes the pre-existing ETH
Confirm AvaxAggregator has identical pattern

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Aggregator accumulates 1 ETH from failed operations. Attacker calls swapIn with $1 worth of tokens, receives ~$1 ETH from swap. Deposit sends entire aggregator balance (~1.001 ETH) to THORChain under attacker's memo, netting ~1 ETH profit.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of all pre-existing ETH/AVAX in the aggregator

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for swapIn with pre-existing aggregator balance

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal token balance for DEX swap
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing swap output with deposit amount

threats.title: Router _routerDeposit safeApprove Incompatible with USDT Approve Race Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: The _routerDeposit function calls safeApprove(asset, router, amount) without first resetting the approval to 0. USDT's approve() function requires the current allowance to be 0 before setting a new non-zero value. If any stale allowance exists (from a partially failed previous migration or deliberate griefing), USDT migration via _routerDeposit will permanently revert, blocking vault churn for USDT.
threats.investigation_rationale: USDT is explicitly mentioned in the project documentation as a supported token. The router is expected to handle 'weird' ERC20 behaviors. _routerDeposit is critical for vault churn and router migration. A blocked migration could strand USDT funds indefinitely.
deployment_assumptions: USDT or similar approve-race-protected tokens are used with the router
threats.config_evidence: USDT.sol in scope; project documentation explicitly states support for USDT-like tokens including 'non_standard_return' and blocklist behaviors

proof_of_signal.lines: _routerDeposit: `safeApprove(_asset, _router, _amount)` - single approve call without reset to 0
proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing approve(0) before approve(amount) for USDT compatibility

preconditions: Token is USDT or similar with approve race protection
Any stale allowance exists from router to new router address
Vault calls transferAllowance or returnVaultAssets with router != address(this)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: safeApprove does approve(router, amount) without first doing approve(router, 0)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: USDT approve reverts if current allowance != 0 and new value != 0
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No approve reset to 0; no special handling for USDT-like tokens

analysis_questions: Can stale allowances accumulate from partial failures?
Could an attacker deliberately create a stale allowance to block migration?
Does AvaxRouter have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Test _routerDeposit with USDT mock that enforces approve(0) requirement
Test with pre-existing non-zero allowance to new router
Verify AvaxRouter _routerDeposit has same pattern

prerequisites: Stale allowance exists or is created

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete inability to migrate USDT during vault churn
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Would require deploying a new migration path

threats.title: AvaxAggregator swapIn safeApprove Reverts on Non-Zero to Non-Zero Approval
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxAggregator uses OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20.safeApprove which reverts if the current allowance is non-zero and the new value is also non-zero. If a previous swapIn left a stale approval (e.g., due to fee-on-transfer where _safeAmount < amount, leaving leftover approval), subsequent swapIn calls for the same token will revert permanently.
threats.investigation_rationale: The swapIn function calls `IERC20(token).safeApprove(address(swapRouter), amount)` using the original `amount` not the `safeAmount` (actual received). For fee-on-transfer tokens, `safeAmount < amount`, so the DEX swap uses `safeAmount` but the full `amount` was approved. The leftover approval (amount - safeAmount) persists, causing the next safeApprove to revert.
deployment_assumptions: Fee-on-transfer tokens are used with AvaxAggregator
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states protocol expects to handle fee-on-transfer tokens

proof_of_signal.lines: swapIn: `IERC20(token).safeApprove(address(swapRouter), amount)` - uses amount not safeAmount
proof_of_signal.pattern: OZ safeApprove reverts on non-zero to non-zero; stale approval from fee-on-transfer tokens

preconditions: Fee-on-transfer token is used
Previous swapIn left stale approval due to amount > safeAmount mismatch
Subsequent swapIn call for same token

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: safeApprove(amount) where amount != safeAmount for fee-on-transfer tokens
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Leftover approval makes future safeApprove calls revert

analysis_questions: Which fee-on-transfer tokens are supported on Avalanche?
Does THORChain_Aggregator have the same issue? (It does approve(amount) not safeApprove)
Can this be triggered by normal operations?
validation_requirements: Test swapIn with fee-on-transfer token mock
Verify stale approval persists after first swap
Confirm second swapIn reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User calls swapIn with a 1% fee-on-transfer token. swapRouter consumes safeAmount but amount was approved. 1% leftover approval persists. Next user calling swapIn for same token gets permanent revert, DoS-ing that token's swap path.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS of swapIn for specific fee-on-transfer tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users trying to swap affected tokens through AvaxAggregator

threats.test_coverage_gap: No fee-on-transfer token tests for AvaxAggregator

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable when swapIn transactions start reverting

threats.title: AvaxRouter deposit() Is Public, Bypassing Expiry Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxRouter's deposit() function is public, unlike THORChain_Router where _deposit is private (only accessible through depositWithExpiry). Users or contracts calling deposit() directly on AvaxRouter bypass the expiration check, allowing stale transactions to execute at unfavorable times. THORChain_Router V5 deliberately made _deposit private to enforce expiry.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an inconsistency between the two router implementations. The THORChain_Router was updated to make _deposit private specifically to enforce the expiry requirement per the stated invariant. AvaxRouter was not updated to match, creating a security gap.
threats.config_evidence: deploy.ts deploys AvaxRouter; hardhat.config.ts targets Avalanche C-Chain (chainId 43114)

proof_of_signal.lines: deposit() is `public payable nonReentrant` vs THORChain_Router._deposit is `private nonReentrant`
proof_of_signal.pattern: Public deposit function bypasses expiration enforcement

preconditions: User or contract calls deposit() directly on AvaxRouter
Transaction is delayed past intended expiration

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Stale deposits can execute at unfavorable rates
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No expiration parameter on deposit(); should be private like THORChain_Router

prerequisites: Direct call to deposit() instead of depositWithExpiry

threats.title: Permissionless Event Spoofing via Dust Deposits Tricks Bifrost Observation
threats.hypothesis_description: Any address can call depositWithExpiry to emit Deposit events with arbitrary vault addresses and memos for minimal cost. For ETH deposits, sending 1 wei emits a legitimate Deposit event. For ERC20 deposits, any token with approval emits the event. If Bifrost's log parser doesn't strictly validate minimum thresholds, vault whitelist, and memo semantics, crafted events could trigger unintended cross-chain actions or create noise disrupting observation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly flags log spoofing as a risk area. The router has no on-chain access control for deposits. The author-flagged risk mentions 'Malicious smart contracts tricking the Bifrost smartcontract_log_parser into registering a fake DepositEvent'. The defense relies entirely on Bifrost off-chain validation.

proof_of_signal.lines: depositWithExpiry/deposit: `emit Deposit(vault, asset, safeAmount, memo)` - no access control on who can emit
proof_of_signal.pattern: Permissionless event emission with arbitrary parameters

preconditions: Attacker has minimal ETH or approved ERC20 tokens
Bifrost's log parser processes events from any sender

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No sender validation for deposit event emission
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can emit Deposit events with crafted memos to any vault address
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum deposit amount, no sender whitelist on-chain

analysis_questions: Does Bifrost validate minimum deposit amounts per asset?
Does Bifrost verify the depositor address or only the event source contract?
Can crafted memos trigger unintended THORChain L1 actions?
validation_requirements: Emit dust Deposit events with various memos and verify Bifrost handling
Check if max_contract_tx_logs limit prevents log flooding attacks
Verify minimum threshold enforcement in Bifrost

threats.title: Any account can spoof vault-originated TransferAllowance events with ETH asset (amount=0), potentially confusing off-chain observers and cross-chain accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: Because transferAllowance() has no vault whitelist/on-chain auth and allows asset==address(0) with amount==0, any EOA can emit TransferAllowance events that appear to originate from a THORChain vault rotation flow. If Bifrost/THORNode off-chain logic relies on the event stream (or memo parsing) without strictly authenticating msg.sender as an active vault, an attacker can inject misleading churn/allowance-move signals.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A highlights event integrity as a reputation/consensus asset. transferAllowance emits a high-signal event with memo. Even though amount=0 avoids allowance underflow, the event still looks like an allowance move. This is a realistic on-chain action with no special privileges needed; the impact depends on off-chain validation rigor.
deployment_assumptions: Bifrost/THORNode consumes TransferAllowance events (or memo fields) in a way that can influence state/accounting without strict sender-vault verification
Off-chain filters do not enforce amount>0 and/or do not validate that msg.sender is a currently active vault
threats.config_evidence: No off-chain parser code provided; protocol docs emphasize event observation and log spoofing risk but do not show specific TransferAllowance handling.

proof_of_signal.pattern: transferAllowance() external nonReentrant; if router==this calls _adjustAllowances; emits TransferAllowance(msg.sender,...). No vault whitelist; amount can be 0; asset can be address(0)

preconditions: Attacker can send a transaction to transferAllowance()
Attacker sets router==address(this) to avoid external calls and uses amount==0 to avoid underflow
Off-chain systems use TransferAllowance events without strict msg.sender vault validation

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless emission of high-signal event used for protocol operations
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: TransferAllowance event can be emitted by any address with arbitrary memo, even with amount=0 and asset=0
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No on-chain vault registry / no restriction that msg.sender is an Asgard/Ygg vault

threats.title: Unbounded loop in batchTransferOutV5 enables caller-driven out-of-gas DoS for vault outbounds when batch size is large
threats.hypothesis_description: batchTransferOutV5 iterates over an unbounded calldata array. A large payload can exceed block gas limits, reverting the entire batch and preventing processing of legitimate transfers within that batch. If operational tooling relies on batching for liveness/cost, this becomes a practical DoS vector (especially if an attacker can influence the batch contents off-chain or if vault software accidentally constructs large batches).
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A flags unbounded iteration as a liveness risk (INV-STATE-014). Even without a public mempool/MEV, a large batch can be submitted on-chain and fail deterministically. Practicality depends on who submits batches (vaults), but the defect is in code and should be bounded or segmented off-chain.
deployment_assumptions: Vault automation submits batchTransferOutV5 transactions and may construct batches from inputs that could be adversarially large or operationally unbounded
threats.config_evidence: No batching limits present on-chain; Phase A notes reliance on off-chain gas budgeting ('bifrost to budget gas limits').

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i=0; i < transferOutPayload.length; ++i) {_transferOutV5(...)}; no max length guard

preconditions: A batchTransferOutV5 transaction is used for operational outbounds
Batch size can become large enough to exceed gas limits
No fallback path automatically splits the batch and retries

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: single revert blocks all transfers in the batch
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum batch size enforced on-chain

analysis_questions: What is the maximum safe batch size under target chain gas limits?
Do vault tools enforce a hard batch cap?
Is there an alternative single-transfer path used on failure?
validation_requirements: Gas benchmark _transferOutV5 for ETH and ERC20 paths, derive max safe length
Review vault/bifrost batching logic for caps and backoff strategy

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If an attacker can cause many pending outbounds to be aggregated into one batch (e.g., via many small swaps), and vault tooling naively batches all, the resulting batchTransferOutV5 reverts repeatedly, delaying outbounds until manual intervention/splitting.
prerequisites: Off-chain influence on batch construction (protocol usage patterns), or operational misconfiguration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users awaiting outbound settlement; vault operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High via operational splitting, but causes liveness incidents

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Yes: per-item gas and worst-case recipient/token behaviors

threats.title: Public vaultAllowance() may leak sensitive operational balances enabling targeted griefing of specific vaults/tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: vaultAllowance(vault, token) exposes per-vault internal allowances publicly. While not a direct on-chain exploit, it can enable attackers to identify high-value vault-token pairs and target those recipients/aggregators with griefing contracts (e.g., contracts that reject ETH, tokens that revert on transfer, or liquidity routes that fail), increasing operational failure rates.
threats.investigation_rationale: Routers rely on off-chain correctness and operational stability. Public disclosure of allowance distribution can make attack planning cheaper and more precise. This is practical in adversarial environments even without mempool MEV, since the data is on-chain state.
deployment_assumptions: Attackers can use observed allowances to choose targets for griefing (e.g., known outbound recipients/aggregators), increasing failure probability
threats.config_evidence: vaultAllowance is public view and returns internal accounting; no mention of obfuscation in docs.

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: leaks vault-token balances/allowances that could be operationally sensitive

threats.title: Reentrancy-adjacent cross-contract call risk: THORChain_Aggregator.rescueFunds uses low-level transfer without return-value decoding, enabling silent failure for non-standard ERC20s
threats.hypothesis_description: rescueFunds() performs a low-level call to token.transfer(destination,amount) and only checks the call-level success boolean, not decoding a returned boolean. For ERC20 tokens that return false (without reverting) on failure, rescueFunds may report success even though no tokens moved. This can strand funds and create inconsistent operational assumptions during incident response.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect pattern: low-level call without decoding return data. The project otherwise uses the safer pattern in routers (success && (data.length==0 || decode(bool))). In an emergency, rescueFunds is likely used, so correctness matters.

proof_of_signal.pattern: asset.call(abi.encodeWithSignature('transfer(address,uint256)',destination,amount)); require(success,'Transfer failed'); does not decode return bool

preconditions: Aggregator holds ERC20 tokens requiring rescue (stuck funds scenario)

Owner uses rescueFunds assuming require(success) implies transfer succeeded

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: false-returning tokens can make rescue appear successful when it failed

threats.title: Permissionless swapOutV5 Allows Draining ERC20 Tokens Stuck in Aggregator
threats.hypothesis_description: THORChain_Aggregator.swapOutV5 is public with no access control. When called with fromAsset != address(0), it approves the swapRouter to spend fromAmount of fromAsset from the aggregator's own balance, then calls swapExactTokensForETH. Any ERC20 tokens stuck in the aggregator (from failed V5 ERC20 swaps per ECON-H002, or any other source) can be drained by anyone calling swapOutV5 with the stuck token as fromAsset and themselves as recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function uses low-level .call to swapRouter, making the aggregator the msg.sender from swapRouter's perspective. Combined with the self-approval, this allows any caller to trigger swaps using the aggregator's token balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control; safeApprove(fromAsset, swapRouter, fromAmount) approves aggregator's own tokens; low-level .call makes aggregator the msg.sender for swapRouter

preconditions: Aggregator holds ERC20 tokens (from failed swaps, direct transfers, etc.)
DEX pool exists for the stuck token paired with WETH
Attacker calls swapOutV5 with fromAsset=stuckToken, fromAmount=aggregator's balance, recipient=attacker

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeApprove(fromAsset, address(swapRouter), fromAmount) uses caller-provided fromAmount against aggregator's balance
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Direct on-chain call to swapOutV5 with attacker-controlled parameters

threats.title: Unbounded Loop in returnVaultAssets Causes Gas Limit DoS During Churn
threats.hypothesis_description: returnVaultAssets iterates over the coins array to transfer allowances for each asset. If a vault has processed transactions for a large number of distinct ERC20 tokens, the array will be large. The gas cost of iterating and updating state could exceed the block gas limit, permanently preventing the vault from returning its assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: Vaults must return assets to complete churn. An attacker could dust the vault with hundreds of distinct tokens to intentionally bloat the coins array and block churn.

threats.title: Outbound Queue Blocking via Reverting Fallback in transferOut
threats.hypothesis_description: In transferOut for ETH/AVAX, if the send to the recipient fails, the router bounces funds back to the vault using .transfer(). If the vault is a smart contract that consumes >2300 gas on receive(), the bounce-back reverts, causing the entire transaction to revert and blocking the outbound queue.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an attacker can intentionally cause an outbound transaction to revert, they can stall the THORChain state machine, as nodes will repeatedly try to process the failing transaction.
deployment_assumptions: Vaults (Asgard/Yggdrasil) are deployed as smart contracts that consume >2300 gas on receive()

threats.title: Router-to-router migration can be hijacked by malicious external router via approval + depositWithExpiry call chain
threats.hypothesis_description: THORChain_Router.transferAllowance/returnVaultAssets allow forwarding assets to an arbitrary `router` address (when router != address(this)) by calling safeApprove(asset, router, amount) and then calling iROUTER(router).depositWithExpiry(...). If the destination `router` is malicious (or not the intended upgraded router), it can use the approval to pull tokens directly and miscredit/never credit the intended vault allowance on the new router, causing permanent loss or mis-accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical operational failure mode during churn/upgrades. Even without off-chain compromise, any misconfiguration of the destination router address (or a maliciously substituted router in an operational process) results in immediate custody loss because the old router grants spend approval before making an external call.

The migrated asset is an ERC20 (approval-based path)
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts only show AvaxRouter/AvaxAggregator deploy; no evidence of router-address governance/verification for migrations in this repo.

threats.title: Arbitrary ETH Drain via transferOutV5 and batchTransferOutV5
threats.hypothesis_description: In `THORChain_Router.sol`, the `_transferOutV5` function uses `transferOutPayload.amount` instead of `msg.value` when transferring ETH. Because `transferOutV5` and `batchTransferOutV5` are public and lack access control or validation that `msg.value == amount`, any attacker can call these functions with `amount` set to the router's entire ETH balance and `msg.value` set to 0, completely draining the router's ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike the V4 `transferOut` which safely uses `msg.value`, the V5 implementation trusts the user-provided `amount` in the payload struct for ETH transfers. This allows unauthenticated callers to spend the contract's own ETH balance.

threats.title: AvaxRouter.returnVaultAssets AVAX Send Failure Reverts Entire Token Migration
threats.hypothesis_description: In returnVaultAssets, AVAX is sent to asgard via `asgard.send(msg.value)` with a hard `require(success)` AFTER all ERC20 allowance adjustments. If the new asgard address cannot receive AVAX (e.g., it's a contract without a receive function, or gas limit of 2300 is insufficient), the entire transaction reverts, rolling back all ERC20 allowance transfers. This blocks the entire vault migration, not just the AVAX component.
threats.investigation_rationale: The AVAX send is at the end of the function after all allowance adjustments. A single failure in AVAX reception blocks all token migrations. This is an ordering/atomicity issue where AVAX handling should not block ERC20 migration.

threats.title: THORChain_Aggregator safeApprove Residual Causes Permanent swapIn DoS for USDT-Like Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn calls safeApprove(token, swapRouter, amount) using the original `amount` parameter, not `_safeAmount` (balance-diff). For fee-on-transfer tokens, _safeAmount < amount, and the DEX swap uses _safeAmount, leaving a residual approval of (amount - _safeAmount). If the token requires approve(0) before non-zero approve (like USDT), subsequent swapIn calls permanently revert because safeApprove doesn't reset to 0 first.
threats.investigation_rationale: The THORChain_Aggregator's internal safeApprove does not zero-reset (unlike swapOutV5's ERC20 branch which does). Combined with the amount/_safeAmount mismatch, this creates a permanent DoS for USDT-like fee-on-transfer tokens on the aggregator's swapIn path.
deployment_assumptions: Token used in swapIn is fee-on-transfer AND requires approve(0) before approve(N)
threats.config_evidence: USDT.sol in scope has basisPointsRate (FoT) and approve race protection. Documentation states 'Any ERC20 token' support.

threats.title: ETH_RUNE transferTo Uses tx.origin Enabling Token Theft via Intermediary Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: ETH_RUNE's transferTo function uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender, allowing any contract in the call chain to transfer tokens from the original transaction signer without their explicit consent or approval. A malicious contract that a user interacts with for any reason can silently drain their entire ETH.RUNE balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a well-documented anti-pattern (SWC-115). The code comment acknowledges the risk but dismisses it as equivalent to infinite approvals. However, unlike infinite approvals, this requires zero user consent - any interaction with any malicious contract suffices. The documentation says ETH.RUNE is deprecated and out of scope, but the code is in the auditable contracts list.

User interacts with any contract (even unrelated to RUNE)
That contract calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo(attackerAddress, amount)

threats.title: TetherToken.deprecate can redirect user transfers/approvals to an arbitrary upgraded contract without interface enforcement, enabling behavioral breaks and potential fund loss
threats.hypothesis_description: USDT-style TetherToken supports an application-level upgrade switch via deprecated/upgradedAddress; when deprecated is true, transfer/transferFrom/approve/balanceOf/allowance/totalSupply calls are forwarded to the upgraded contract. There is no on-chain enforcement that upgradedAddress implements the expected semantics, nor any migration of balances. A malicious owner (or compromised owner key) could set upgradedAddress to a contract that behaves adversarially (e.g., steals balances, lies on balanceOf), breaking user expectations and potentially enabling value extraction in integrated protocols.
threats.investigation_rationale: While admin abuse is low realism under the threat model (requires owner compromise/collusion), this is a high-impact control point and a common real-world failure mode. Many protocols integrating USDT-like tokens may not anticipate deprecate redirection risks in internal accounting.

Upgraded contract is malicious or incompatible

threats.title: THORChain_Aggregator rescueFunds Enables Owner Extraction of ERC20s Stuck from Failed V5 Swaps

threats.title: THORChain_Router.transferAllowance External Router Path Creates Stale Token Approvals
threats.hypothesis_description: When transferAllowance is called with router != address(this), _routerDeposit approves the external router for tokens and calls its depositWithExpiry. If the external router's depositWithExpiry succeeds but does not actually pull tokens via transferFrom (e.g., a malicious contract that accepts the call but does nothing), the approval remains active. The external address retains permanent approval to pull tokens from the THORChain_Router at any future time.

threats.title: AvaxRouter.transferOutAndCall Fallback Sends to Recipient But Event Data Enables Log Spoofing
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxRouter.transferOutAndCall is public payable with no access control for the ETH/AVAX path. Any address can call it with msg.value, causing a TransferOutAndCall event where msg.sender appears as the vault. If the aggregator call fails, AVAX falls through to the recipient. The emitted event contains attacker-chosen parameters (target, finalToken, to, amountOutMin) which Bifrost observes. A malicious caller could craft events that confuse Bifrost's transaction parser if vault address validation is insufficient.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function has no on-chain access control for AVAX transfers (no allowance check). While the economic cost to the attacker equals msg.value, the ability to emit TransferOutAndCall events from arbitrary addresses could create confusion in Bifrost's outbound tracking, potentially causing THORChain L1 to process fake outbound completions.
